passages.context: Old Major is the oldest pig on the farm. He calls all the animals together one night to give a big speech. He tells them their lives are bad because of the humans, and that the animals need to take over. This is the speech that starts the whole revolution.
tokens: Man is the only creature that consumes without producing.
He does not give milk, he does not lay eggs,
he is too weak to pull the plough,
he cannot run fast enough to catch rabbits.
Yet he is lord of all the animals.
He sets them to work,
he gives back to them the bare minimum that will prevent them from starving,
and the rest he keeps for himself.

expertMarks.justification: This is the big idea behind the Russian Revolution: rich owners eat well while poor workers do all the work. Orwell is saying it through a pig, but he means real bosses and real workers.
The word 'lord' is what people called kings and rich landowners. Orwell uses it to remind us of the Tsar (the king of Russia) who the revolution overthrew.
This is a simple version of a famous idea by Karl Marx: bosses take more than they give back. Old Major is teaching the animals revolution in plain words.
Old Major puts milk and eggs first. These come from FEMALE bodies (cows and hens). The whole speech leans on women's work, but it doesn't really notice.
'Lord' is a male word. Old Major imagines the enemy as one big man. He doesn't ask whether male animals will treat female animals fairly after the revolution.
Old Major splits the world in two: the people who MAKE things (workers) and the people who EAT things (owners). That is the whole rule about power in one sentence.
Look at the pronouns. 'He' (the boss) gives the orders. 'Them' (the workers) do as they're told. Two tiny words; two different jobs.
The boss gives the workers JUST enough food to keep them alive -- nothing more. Orwell is saying this is how the rich keep the poor: barely fed, barely free.
What is taken is more than what is given back. That gap is where the boss's profit comes from. Eight short words explain a huge idea.
The first line is short and certain. It makes you nod along. Orwell wants you to feel the pull of a big speech BEFORE he shows you how speeches like this can be misused later.
The word 'Yet' is a hinge. It twists your feelings: humans seem useless, BUT they're in charge -- which feels unfair. Orwell wants you to feel that anger.

passages.reference: Chapter 3 -- Squealer on the milk and apples
passages.context: After the animals take over, the milk and the windfall apples (apples that have fallen from the trees) go missing. The animals find out the pigs are keeping them all. Squealer, the talking pig, is sent to explain WHY the pigs deserve them. This is the first time we hear pig propaganda.
tokens: Comrades!
You do not imagine, I hope, that we pigs are doing this in a spirit of selfishness and privilege?
Many of us actually dislike milk and apples.
I dislike them myself.
Our sole object in taking these things is to preserve our health.
Milk and apples (this has been proved by Science, comrades) contain substances absolutely necessary to the well-being of a pig.
We pigs are brainworkers.
Day and night we are watching over your welfare.
It is for your sake that we drink that milk and eat those apples.
Do you know what would happen if we pigs failed in our duty? Jones would come back!

expertMarks.justification: 'Proved by Science' is what Soviet leaders said when they wanted you to believe them. Squealer uses 'science' the way Stalin used 'science' -- to win arguments without being checked.
Squealer scares them with 'Jones would come back!' Real Soviet leaders did the same: 'Don't complain, or the bad old times will return.' Fear stops people questioning.

'Brainworker' makes the pigs sound special -- like they think with their heads, not their bodies. After the Russian Revolution, the new bosses gave themselves nicer jobs and more food while everyone else worked the land. Same trick here.

The pigs (mostly imagined as male) call themselves 'brainworkers'. Animals like Clover (a female workhorse) and the hens do the hard physical work. Brain = male. Body = female. That split is hidden in the word.

Squealer puts the suspicion in the animals' mouths so he can deny it. The animals he is taking food FROM include the cows (who give the milk) and the hens (who lay the eggs). Their voices are missing here.
Watch the trick: he says 'You do not IMAGINE...' That tells the animals their suspicion is silly before they even speak. This is how the new ruling class shuts down questions.
A new word -- 'brainworkers' -- creates a new class. The pigs are no longer just animals. They are a special group who deserve more. Watch a class being born in real time.
Squealer turns being greedy into being generous. He says the pigs eat the food FOR the workers. The bosses always pretend their privileges help everyone.
'Comrades!' tries to make you feel like one of the team. But you, the reader, can hear it differently from the animals. You're invited in -- but you can also see through it.
'I dislike them myself.' This is Squealer trying to seem honest. You can spot the trick BECAUSE Orwell makes it so obvious. The animals can't see it. You can.
The exclamation mark, the question, the threat -- all stacked together. Orwell is teaching you what scary politics sounds like, so you'll spot it again later.

passages.context: Things are going badly on the farm. Food is running out. Napoleon (the new pig leader) decides to sell 400 eggs a week to humans, to buy food. The hens say no. This is the only time anyone really fights back against Napoleon.
tokens: When the hens heard this, they raised a terrible outcry.
They had been warned earlier that this sacrifice might be necessary,
but had not believed that it would really happen.
They were just getting their clutches ready for the spring sitting,
and they protested that to take the eggs away now was murder.
For the first time since the expulsion of Jones, there was something resembling a rebellion.
Led by three young Black Minorca pullets,
the hens made a determined effort to thwart Napoleon's wishes.

expertMarks.justification: Stalin took grain from Russian farmers and called it 'sacrifice for the future'. Millions starved. Orwell knew this. The hens' eggs are a small picture of a huge real-life cruelty.

The word 'rebellion' was used for the GOOD revolt against Jones. Now it is used for an animal protest AGAINST the pig leaders. Same word, different team. Orwell shows that 'rebellion' depends on who's in charge.
Orwell names the breed of hen on purpose. Stalin had real opposition leaders killed and erased from history. Orwell gives these hens a name so we won't forget them.
The hens act together. Orwell says they 'raised a terrible outcry' -- not 'shrieked' or 'made a fuss'. Female protest is taken seriously. That is a quiet feminist choice by Orwell.

The hens have been planning their families. Their eggs aren't just food -- they're future chicks. Orwell shows female bodies doing real work. The pigs see eggs as products. The hens see eggs as their kids.
The hens don't say 'sacrifice' (the regime's word). They say 'murder'. They name what is happening to them. Take their words seriously -- they are the ones it's happening to.
Saying 'we warned you' is a trick. It tells the hens their pain is their own fault. The 'sacrifice' is the hens'. The 'necessary' is Napoleon's. Pigs make the rules; hens pay the cost.
Napoleon calls it 'a contract'. The hens call it 'murder'. WHO names something is who controls it. Refusing the boss's word is itself an act of power.
Orwell calls Napoleon's rules 'wishes'. That's a small word. It says: this is one pig's preference being forced on everybody. The hens treat his 'wishes' as something they can ignore.
'Terrible' has two meanings here. Terrible for the hens (their eggs are taken). Terrible for Napoleon (the hens won't shut up). You feel both at once.
The word 'murder' in a children's book about animals is shocking. Orwell is doing it on purpose, to make you stop and notice that something serious is happening.
'For the first time since...' tells you to compare moments. You're being asked to see the pattern: the new bosses now have an opposition, just like the old bosses did. That recognition IS the lesson.

passages.context: Years have passed. The pigs have become rich. They throw a dinner party for the human farmers next door -- the same humans they once kicked out. The other animals watch through the farmhouse window. This is the very last scene of the book.
tokens: What was it that had altered in the faces of the pigs?
Clover's old dim eyes flitted from one face to another.
Some of them had five chins, some had four, some had three.
But what was it that seemed to be melting and changing?
Twelve voices were shouting in anger,
and they were all alike.
The creatures outside looked from pig to man, and from man to pig, and from pig to man again;
but already it was impossible to say which was which.

expertMarks.justification: By 1945, Stalin was meeting with Churchill and Roosevelt -- the leaders of the West he once attacked. Orwell saw the revolution had joined the world it promised to destroy. 'Twelve voices' = old enemies now sharing a table.
Pig-to-man-to-pig-to-man. The rhythm itself shows the swap happening. Orwell's view: the new bosses had become the old bosses. Russia had become the thing it fought.
'Already' is the punch. Orwell is saying: this swap happened FAST. The animals didn't even notice until it was too late. That is his warning to the reader -- and to you.

Clover sees what's happening. She is old, female, a workhorse, and a mother who lost her foals at the start. Orwell trusts the most worn-out body to be the wisest witness. But she still has no voice -- only eyes.
Five chins, four chins, three chins. The pigs are now FAT -- they look like rich human men. Their bodies show what they took from the working bodies (Clover, Boxer) outside. Class shows up as flesh.
Power has a body: fat. The pigs got fat from the food they took from the workers. You can SEE who took what.
Old Major said 'Man is the enemy'. Now the pigs ARE the men. Orwell wipes out the old line between animals and humans. There is only one line left: rulers and ruled.
You can't tell pig from man. The names don't matter -- pig, man, boss. Only the chair they sit in matters. Power stays. The names change.
Clover asks the question. You ask it with her. Orwell makes YOU work out what's happening. He doesn't tell you -- he points and lets you see it.
'Melting and changing' is what your eyes do when you can't trust what you see. Orwell wants you to feel confused -- because the animals outside the window feel confused too.
Read this line out loud. Your head turns left, right, left, right. Orwell makes your body act out Clover's looking. You're not just reading about her -- you're doing what she's doing.
The book ends without comfort. No happy ending, no answer. Orwell wants you to walk away worried -- because you should be.
